title: Relative & Participle Clauses — Band 7+ Reference
subtitle: Defining / Non-defining / Reduced / Participle clauses — the elegance of Band 7+ writing
intro: Relative and participle clauses let you pack two ideas into one sentence elegantly. Band 6 writers say 'A man came in. He was wearing a blue jacket.' Band 7+ writers say 'A man wearing a blue jacket came in.' Mastering these structures is the single biggest source of the 'sophistication' examiners reward in Lexical Resource and Grammatical Range.

sections.title: 1. Relative Pronouns — Which One to Use
sections.summary: The relative pronoun replaces a noun in a second clause and links the two clauses together.

examples.plural: The student WHO scored highest received a scholarship.
The teacher WHO(M) I admire most has retired.

rules.pattern: WHICH — for things and animals (subject or object)

examples.plural: The book WHICH won the prize is a novel.
The course WHICH I took last year was excellent.

rules.pattern: THAT — for people OR things (defining clauses only)

examples.plural: The student THAT scored highest received a scholarship.
The book THAT won the prize is a novel.

rules.note: 'That' cannot be used in non-defining clauses (those with commas). And 'that' is more informal than 'who/which' in writing.

examples.plural: The author WHOSE book won the prize is from India.
I live in a city WHOSE population is over ten million.

rules.note: Many candidates avoid 'whose' for things and write awkward 'of which' constructions. 'Whose' is fully accepted for non-human nouns and reads more naturally.

examples.plural: This is the city WHERE I was born.
I remember the day WHEN we first met.
The reason WHY he left is unclear.

rules.note: Alternatives: 'the city in which', 'the day on which', 'the reason for which' — same meaning, more formal.

sections.title: 2. Defining vs Non-Defining — The Comma Rule
sections.summary: The single most important relative-clause distinction. Punctuation completely changes the meaning.

rules.pattern: DEFINING — identifies WHICH ONE — NO commas

examples.plural: The student who scored highest got the prize. (= specifies WHICH student)
Books that are written for children are usually short.
The book I read yesterday was fascinating. (= which I read)

rules.note: If you remove the relative clause, the meaning is lost or unclear. 'The student got the prize' alone — which student?

rules.pattern: NON-DEFINING — adds extra info — COMMAS required

examples.plural: My brother, who lives in Canada, is a doctor. (assumes you know which brother)
Bangladesh, which has a population of 170 million, is one of the most densely populated countries.
I met the new manager, who turned out to be very approachable.

rules.note: Remove the relative clause and the sentence still makes complete sense: 'My brother is a doctor.'

rules.pattern: Meaning shift between defining and non-defining

examples.plural: My sister who lives in Dhaka is a teacher. (= I have multiple sisters; this one specifically)
My sister, who lives in Dhaka, is a teacher. (= I have one sister; she lives in Dhaka)

examples.singular: ❌ My brother, that lives in Canada, is a doctor.
examples.plural: ✔ My brother, who lives in Canada, is a doctor.

sections.tip: Test: read the sentence WITHOUT the relative clause. If the meaning is still clear → use commas (non-defining). If meaning becomes vague → no commas (defining).

sections.title: 3. Relative Clauses With Prepositions
sections.summary: Two ways to handle prepositions in relative clauses — formal vs informal. Band 7+ writing tends toward the formal placement.

rules.pattern: Preposition at the END (informal, more common in speech)

examples.plural: The man who(m) I spoke to is a lawyer.
The book that I was looking for is on the table.
The topic which we discussed yesterday is fascinating.

rules.pattern: Preposition BEFORE the relative pronoun (formal, written)

examples.plural: The man TO WHOM I spoke is a lawyer.
The book FOR WHICH I was looking is on the table.
The topic ABOUT WHICH we discussed yesterday is fascinating. ❌ — see note

rules.note: When preposition comes before, you MUST use 'whom' (not who) and 'which' (not that). 'That' cannot follow a preposition: ❌ 'the book for that I was looking'. ALSO, the third example is incorrect because 'discuss' is not followed by 'about' — say 'discussed' alone.

examples.plural: There were 50 students, ten of WHOM had failed the exam.
The committee made several proposals, most of WHICH were accepted.
The book has 12 chapters, the first of WHICH is an introduction.

rules.note: Highly academic. One per essay is impressive.

sections.title: 4. Reduced Relative Clauses — Compactness
sections.summary: Drop 'who/which/that + be' to compress the sentence. Band 7+ writing is full of these — they sound more elegant than full relative clauses.

examples.singular: Full: People who live in cities
examples.plural: Reduced: People living in cities

examples.singular: Full: Students who are studying abroad
examples.plural: Reduced: Students studying abroad

examples.singular: Full: The man who is sitting in the corner
examples.plural: Reduced: The man sitting in the corner

examples.singular: Full: A noise which is coming from the engine
examples.plural: Reduced: A noise coming from the engine

examples.singular: Full: A book which was written by Orwell
examples.plural: Reduced: A book written by Orwell

examples.singular: Full: The proposal that was submitted yesterday
examples.plural: Reduced: The proposal submitted yesterday

examples.singular: Full: Houses which are built of wood
examples.plural: Reduced: Houses built of wood

examples.singular: Full: People who are affected by the policy
examples.plural: Reduced: People affected by the policy

examples.singular: Full: The applicants who are eligible for the scholarship
examples.plural: Reduced: The applicants eligible for the scholarship

examples.singular: Full: Issues which are relevant to the topic
examples.plural: Reduced: Issues relevant to the topic
Students who must attend the workshop... (can't reduce — 'must' is essential)
People who know the answer... (don't reduce stative verbs to -ing)
The CEO, who has worked here for 20 years, will retire next month. (reduction possible but less natural)

sections.tip: Take any 250-word essay you've written. Find every 'who is / which is / that are / which was / who have'. Reduce as many as possible. Your essay will instantly read more sophisticated.

sections.title: 5. Participle Clauses — Adverbial Compactness
sections.summary: Participle clauses replace adverbial clauses (when, because, after) with a verb form. Even more compact than reduced relatives. Band 7+ examiners specifically reward these.

examples.singular: Full: While I was walking down the street, I saw a fox.
examples.plural: Reduced: Walking down the street, I saw a fox.

examples.singular: Full: Because she was tired, she went to bed early.
examples.plural: Reduced: Being tired, she went to bed early.

examples.singular: Full: As we look at the data, we can see…
examples.plural: Reduced: Looking at the data, we can see…

examples.singular: Full: Since it was built in 1850, the bridge has stood here.
examples.plural: Reduced: Built in 1850, the bridge has stood here.

examples.singular: Full: Because she was disappointed by the result, she withdrew.
examples.plural: Reduced: Disappointed by the result, she withdrew.

examples.singular: Full: When confronted with such evidence, the minister had to resign.
examples.plural: Reduced: Confronted with such evidence, the minister had to resign.

examples.singular: Full: After he had finished his work, he left the office.
examples.plural: Reduced: Having finished his work, he left the office.

examples.singular: Full: After we had considered the evidence, we reached a decision.
examples.plural: Reduced: Having considered the evidence, we reached a decision.

examples.singular: Full: Because she had lived abroad for years, she spoke fluent English.
examples.plural: Reduced: Having lived abroad for years, she spoke fluent English.

rules.note: 'Having + V3' is the highest-yield Band 7+ participle. It signals careful sequencing of past events.

rules.pattern: HAVING BEEN + V3 (Perfect passive participle) — formal sequence

examples.singular: Full: After he had been informed of the decision, he wrote a reply.
examples.plural: Reduced: Having been informed of the decision, he wrote a reply.

sections.tip: Drop ONE 'Having + V3' or 'Having been + V3' into a Task 2 essay. It is the single highest-impact compaction structure for Band 7.5+.

sections.title: 6. Dangling Participles — The Critical Trap
sections.summary: The participle's implicit subject MUST match the main clause's subject. Mismatching them creates absurd sentences — and is one of the most-noticed style errors at Band 7+.

examples.singular: ❌ dangling: Walking down the street, the rain started.
examples.plural: (Logically: the rain is walking — absurd)

examples.singular: ✔ fixed: Walking down the street, I noticed the rain start.
examples.plural: (I am walking — matches)

examples.singular: ❌ dangling: Built in 1850, I admired the old bridge.
examples.plural: (Logically: I was built in 1850 — absurd)

examples.singular: ✔ fixed: Built in 1850, the old bridge stands as a landmark.
examples.plural: (The bridge was built — matches)

rules.pattern: More dangling examples in Task 2 register

examples.singular: ❌ Living in a fast-paced society, technology has become indispensable.
examples.plural: ✔ Living in a fast-paced society, people increasingly rely on technology.

examples.singular: ❌ Faced with rising prices, the government raised interest rates.
examples.plural: ✔ Faced with rising prices, consumers cut back on spending. (the consumers face the prices, not the government)

examples.singular: ❌ Considering the evidence, a decision must be reached.
examples.plural: ✔ Considering the evidence, the committee must reach a decision.

rules.pattern: Quick check: read the participle aloud, then the subject

examples.plural: WHO is walking? → must be the subject of the main clause
WHO is faced? → must be the subject of the main clause
WHO has finished? → must be the subject of the main clause

sections.tip: After writing any sentence beginning with a participle (-ing, -ed, having + V3), find the subject of the main clause. Ask: 'Is the SUBJECT really the one doing the participle action?' If not, rewrite.

sections.title: 7. Relative & Participle Clauses in IELTS Writing
sections.summary: Specific contexts where these structures earn marks.

rules.pattern: Task 1 — describing data with relative/participle compaction

examples.plural: The country which experienced the largest growth was India. → The country experiencing the largest growth was India.
Sales, which had been declining since 2010, recovered sharply in 2015.
Once heated, the mixture is poured into moulds. (reduced: Once it is heated)

rules.pattern: Task 2 — adding context with non-defining relatives

examples.plural: Public transport, which is heavily subsidised in many cities, plays a crucial role in reducing traffic congestion.
Climate change, which has emerged as the defining challenge of our time, demands collective action.

rules.pattern: Task 2 introduction — opening with a participle phrase

examples.plural: Faced with rising global temperatures, governments are under increasing pressure to act.
Living in an age of unprecedented technological change, we are constantly forced to adapt.

rules.pattern: Task 2 conclusion — Having + V3 for summing up

examples.plural: Having considered both perspectives, it seems clear that…
Having weighed the evidence, one cannot avoid the conclusion that…
Walking through the streets of my hometown, I always feel a sense of nostalgia.
Faced with such challenges, young people today need stronger support systems.

sections.title: 8. Top Relative & Participle Clause Errors at the Band 6 → 7+ Threshold
sections.summary: Specific mistakes that reveal Band 6 grammar — and how to fix them.

examples.singular: ❌ My father, that is a teacher, lives in Dhaka.
examples.plural: ✔ My father, who is a teacher, lives in Dhaka.

examples.singular: ❌ My sister who lives in Dhaka is a teacher (when there is only one sister)
examples.plural: ✔ My sister, who lives in Dhaka, is a teacher. (commas because there is only one)

rules.pattern: 3. Adding redundant subject pronoun in relative clause

examples.singular: ❌ The book that I bought IT yesterday is fascinating.
examples.plural: ✔ The book that I bought yesterday is fascinating. (no 'it' — the relative pronoun replaces it)

examples.singular: ❌ People who they live in cities tend to be busier.
examples.plural: ✔ People who live in cities tend to be busier. (no 'they')

rules.pattern: 4. Wrong relative pronoun for people / things

examples.singular: ❌ The person which I admire most…
examples.plural: ✔ The person who(m) I admire most…

examples.singular: ❌ The book who won the prize…
examples.plural: ✔ The book which/that won the prize…

examples.singular: ❌ Walking to school, the rain caught me.
examples.plural: ✔ Walking to school, I was caught by the rain.

examples.singular: ❌ Students attending must wear ID. (intended: Students who must attend)
examples.plural: ✔ Students who must attend the seminar must wear ID. (don't reduce 'must')

examples.singular: awkward: The book the cover of which is torn
examples.plural: natural: The book WHOSE cover is torn

rules.note: 'Whose' for things is fully accepted and reads more naturally.

examples.singular: ❌ People knowing the answer raised their hands.
examples.plural: ✔ People who knew the answer raised their hands. (don't reduce 'know' to 'knowing')

rules.note: Stative verbs (know, believe, own, contain) generally don't reduce to -ing form.

sections.tip: Run a 'WHO/WHICH/THAT/COMMA' audit on any essay. (1) Every 'that' in commas → fix to who/which. (2) Every defining clause WITH commas, check if the noun has only one possible referent. (3) Every relative clause: try reducing it. (4) Every -ing or -ed participle at the start of a sentence: confirm the subject matches.

checklist: I distinguish defining (no commas) from non-defining (commas) relative clauses on every relative clause I write.
I never use 'that' in a non-defining clause (with commas) — only 'who' or 'which'.
I use 'whose' freely for things, not just for people, instead of awkward 'of which' constructions.
I never insert a redundant subject pronoun ('that I bought IT', 'who they live').
I can reduce active relative clauses to -ing forms (people LIVING in cities) and passive ones to -ed forms (a book WRITTEN by Orwell).
I use 'Having + V3' / 'Having been + V3' at least once per Band 7+ Task 2 essay.
I always check that participle clause subjects match the main clause subject (no danglers).
I open at least one Task 2 sentence with a participle phrase ('Faced with…', 'Having considered…', 'Looking at…').
I move prepositions before 'whom' or 'which' (to whom, for which) for formal register at least once per essay.
I use quantifier + 'of whom' / 'of which' (most of which, ten of whom) in academic writing for sophistication.
